pages.title: Custody & Decision-Making Responsibility in Ontario
pages.metaTitle: Custody & Decision-Making Responsibility in Ontario | Our Separation
pages.metaDescription: Learn how custody, decision-making responsibility, and parenting time work in Ontario. Plain-language guidance for separating parents. Create a parenting plan online.

internalLinks.label: Custody vs decision-making: what's the difference?

contentBlocks.h1: Custody and Decision-Making Responsibility in Ontario
contentBlocks.subhead: Ontario has moved away from "custody" language. Today, parenting is about decision-making responsibility and parenting time—built around your child's best interests. Our Separation helps you create a clear parenting plan online, in plain language.

contentBlocks.heading: Why "Custody" Still Shows Up—And What Ontario Law Uses Now
contentBlocks.html:
Many parents search for "custody" because that's the term they've heard for years. In Canadian family law—especially after reforms to the Divorce Act—the focus is now on:

  • Decision-making responsibility (who makes major decisions)
  • Parenting time (when the child is with each parent)

This shift is meant to keep parenting disputes child-centred and reduce "winner/loser" framing. The practical question is not who "gets custody," but how you will make decisions and structure time in a way that supports your child's stability, safety, and development.

contentBlocks.heading: Core Concepts: Decision-Making Responsibility and Parenting Time
contentBlocks.html:
Decision-making responsibility

Decision-making responsibility is authority over major decisions in a child's life, commonly including:

  • Education
  • Health care
  • Religion
  • Significant extracurricular commitments
  • Culture and language

It does not control routine, day-to-day choices made during parenting time.

Parenting time

Parenting time is the time a child spends in each parent's care. During that time, the parent makes day-to-day decisions (meals, bedtime routines, transportation, homework support, etc.).

Parenting time and decision-making responsibility are related but separate—one parent may have more parenting time even if major decision-making is shared, and vice versa.

contentBlocks.heading: Common Arrangements in Ontario
contentBlocks.html:
There is no one-size-fits-all. The best structure depends on the child's needs and the parents' ability to communicate effectively.

Sole decision-making responsibility

One parent has final authority for major decisions. This may be appropriate when cooperation is not realistic, or when stability and clarity are priorities.

Joint decision-making responsibility

Parents must consult and agree on major decisions. This works best where communication is reliable and conflict is low.

Split (divided) decision-making

Each parent controls certain categories (e.g., one handles education, the other health). Less common, but sometimes practical where parents are competent but disagree in specific domains.

Shared parenting time

Often described as "50/50," shared parenting can take different forms. Equal time is not automatic—courts focus on feasibility and the child's best interests.

contentBlocks.heading: How Ontario Determines What's Best for a Child

items.title: Stability and routine
items.html: Children tend to do best when schedules, schooling, and caregiving patterns are predictable.

items.title: The child's needs and development
items.html: Age, temperament, learning needs, health concerns, and emotional well-being matter.

items.title: Parent–child relationships
items.html: The quality of each relationship, and each parent's history of involvement, are relevant.

items.title: Ability to communicate and cooperate
items.html: Some structures (especially joint decision-making) require real collaboration.

items.title: Safety and family violence
items.html: Where safety is an issue, protective measures can override other considerations.

items.title: Willingness to support the other parent's relationship
items.html: Ontario courts take a dim view of gatekeeping and unnecessary interference—subject to safety concerns.

items.aText: Often used for younger children to avoid long separations. Example: Parent A (Mon–Tue), Parent B (Wed–Thu), alternating weekends (Fri–Sun), then flip.
items.aHtml: Often used for younger children to avoid long separations. Example: Parent A (Mon–Tue), Parent B (Wed–Thu), alternating weekends (Fri–Sun), then flip.

items.aText: Common for older children where transitions are manageable. Works best when parents live close and communication is functional.
items.aHtml: Common for older children where transitions are manageable. Works best when parents live close and communication is functional.

items.aText: One home is the primary base, with predictable time for the other parent (e.g., alternating weekends plus one mid-week overnight).
items.aHtml: One home is the primary base, with predictable time for the other parent (e.g., alternating weekends plus one mid-week overnight).

items.aText: Most plans add detailed holiday rules to reduce conflict: winter break splits, alternating statutory holidays, and set vacation notice timelines.
items.aHtml: Most plans add detailed holiday rules to reduce conflict: winter break splits, alternating statutory holidays, and set vacation notice timelines.

contentBlocks.title: Family Violence and High-Conflict Parenting
contentBlocks.html:
Where there are safety concerns, decision-making responsibility and parenting time must be structured to protect the child and the affected parent. Ontario law recognizes that family violence can include coercive control and patterns of intimidation—not only physical harm.

Learn more about family violence and parenting in Ontario →

contentBlocks.heading: What a Strong Parenting Plan Covers

items.title: Decision-making rules
items.html: Define who decides what, when consultation is required, and how disagreements will be addressed.

items.title: Parenting time schedule
items.html: Set a predictable weekly routine, plus holidays, PD days, birthdays, and vacations.

items.title: Communication
items.html: Set expectations for child-related communication (method, response times, boundaries).

items.title: Transitions and logistics
items.html: Pickup/drop-off locations, responsibility for transportation, and rules for late changes.

items.title: Information sharing
items.html: School updates, medical records, extracurricular schedules, and how each parent stays informed.

items.title: Dispute resolution
items.html: A practical escalation path (discussion → written proposal → mediation) before court where appropriate.

contentBlocks.heading: Why Parents Use Our Separation
contentBlocks.html:
Common pain points versus how Our Separation helps:

Common pain pointsHow Our Separation helps
Confusing terms like custody/accessPlain-language Ontario parenting concepts: decision-making and parenting time
Templates that miss key clausesGuided parenting plan flow that prompts for the provisions most families need
High conflict over small issuesBuilt-in structure: schedules, communication rules, and dispute resolution options
Unclear holiday rulesPre-built holiday and vacation modules you can customize
Fear of "getting it wrong"Ontario-specific framing designed for separation agreements and practical implementation

items.q: Is "custody" still a legal term in Ontario?
items.aText: People still use the word "custody," but modern Canadian family law focuses on decision-making responsibility and parenting time. What matters is the substance of your parenting plan, not the label.
items.aHtml: People still use the word "custody," but modern Canadian family law focuses on decision-making responsibility and parenting time. What matters is the substance of your parenting plan, not the label.

items.q: Does joint decision-making mean equal parenting time?
items.aText: No. Decision-making responsibility and parenting time are separate concepts. A parent can share decision-making without having 50/50 time, and a parent can have significant time even if decision-making is not shared.
items.aHtml: No. Decision-making responsibility and parenting time are separate concepts. A parent can share decision-making without having 50/50 time, and a parent can have significant time even if decision-making is not shared.

items.q: Do Ontario courts automatically order 50/50 parenting?
items.aText: No. There is no automatic presumption of equal time. Courts prioritize the child's best interests, including stability, feasibility, communication capacity, and safety.
items.aHtml: No. There is no automatic presumption of equal time. Courts prioritize the child's best interests, including stability, feasibility, communication capacity, and safety.

items.q: Can a parenting plan be changed later?
items.aText: Yes. Parenting terms can be adjusted by agreement or, if necessary, through a court process when circumstances materially change. Many parents build review points into their plans as children grow.
items.aHtml: Yes. Parenting terms can be adjusted by agreement or, if necessary, through a court process when circumstances materially change. Many parents build review points into their plans as children grow.

items.q: Does child support depend on custody labels?
items.aText: Child support is generally driven by income and parenting time arrangements, not by labels like custody.
items.aHtml: Child support is generally driven by income and parenting time arrangements, not by labels like "custody."

contentBlocks.text: If your situation involves safety concerns, consider getting legal advice before finalizing parenting terms. This information is for educational purposes and does not constitute legal advice.

acceptedAnswer.text: People still use the word "custody," but modern Canadian family law focuses on decision-making responsibility and parenting time. What matters is the substance of your parenting plan, not the label.

mainEntity.name: Does joint decision-making mean equal parenting time?

acceptedAnswer.text: No. Decision-making responsibility and parenting time are separate concepts. A parent can share decision-making without having 50/50 time, and a parent can have significant time even if decision-making is not shared.

mainEntity.name: Do Ontario courts automatically order 50/50 parenting?

acceptedAnswer.text: No. There is no automatic presumption of equal time. Courts prioritize the child's best interests, including stability, feasibility, communication capacity, and safety.

mainEntity.name: Can a parenting plan be changed later?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes. Parenting terms can be adjusted by agreement or, if necessary, through a court process when circumstances materially change. Many parents build review points into their plans as children grow.

mainEntity.name: Does child support depend on custody labels?

acceptedAnswer.text: Child support is generally driven by income and parenting time arrangements, not by labels like custody.
